reasoning:
The resolution requires Javokhir Sindarov to be ranked 11th or better on the specified dates. For the first date (April 19, 2026), the official FIDE list will not have updated yet (updates happen universally on the 1st of every month), so the April 1st list maintaining him at 11th will still be current. For May 2026 and beyond, we look at his recent live performance: Sindarov is dominating the Candidates 2026 tournament with 8.5/11 and has pushed his live ranking up by 14 Elo points to 2759, which gives him a live world rank of #6. Barring an apocalyptic 3-loss streak in the last three rounds, he is virtually guaranteed to be in the FIDE top 10 when the May 1 list is released. 

Moving into mid/late 2026, he is likely to participate in high-level closed tournaments and potentially a World Championship match, during which top players generally protect or boost their rating. However, the rating band from 2740-2770 is dense, heavily populated by young rising stars like Gukesh, Firouzja, Praggnanandhaa, and Keymer. Given this density, rank volatility is somewhat high — bad form for a single tournament could cost someone their top-11 status. Despite this volatility, Sindarov is just 20 years old and showing massive breakout strength, making it very probable he stays elite overall. 

Over a multi-year horizon (1 to 10 years), the inherent variance of chess careers (form changes, new prodigies, shifting meta) comes into play. While most prodigies establishing themselves in the top 10 around age 20 maintain a spot for several years, there is enough historical precedent of top-10 players dropping out to temper extreme confidence. In line with the prompt’s guideline, uncertainty properly increases as the forecast horizon extends out to 2036, trending toward 0.5.
